reviews.review_text:
I have 600+ hours b'ween ps4 and ps5 (been playing the game since release in 2017 with crashouts pauses) and 40+ hours on pc; the only way to describe it is a toxic relationship.

For Honor is your ex crazy girlfriend, every time she beat you up, spit in your face, yell at you saying that you're the problem, one day you get tired and leave.

Than she returns, say to you that wants to mend things and get everything perfect this time, and you believe it, in the bottom of your heart you wanna believe it.

And after a short while, you remember the time a centurion ganked you when you were trying to recover stamina, spamming punches on you while a nobushi is doin' the annoying bleeding effect, you try to defend yourself to no avail.

The objective A is almost conquered, maybe your team will regain momentum and the dominium match will not result in another defeat, than out a nowhere a Rider tackle you like a quaterback during the Superball, you stare a the screen seeing the void approaching while falling to the ground, your team's running around without getting a single score base.

The PTSD of a 4 shinobi squad, spamming kicks and teleports like a Naruto Shippuden spin-off.

The incomprensible guttural shouting of the Highlander, the last thing you heard before the Berserk-like massive sword wielded by the Gardener Willy character will get your health bar at half with a single heavy not-parriable hit, even worse if the giant nordic barbarian kicked you against the wall for an easy win;

The 1' prestige Shugoki player, where you have a 50/50 chance: a smurf account of a banned 500 prestiges player or a new soul reclamed by the game sitting a 1.49 on Steam, a bear trap concealed as a gift.

The restart of the relationship is like the release of the Juren, in the ingenuinity of the moment, you really believe a new fresh addition to the formula will spice things up like a new beggining, then as a lovecraftian horror, the spamming and the invicibility effect is even worse then last time, did you really thought the dragonaball-like ultra instinct of the Virtuosa was the worst that the devs could got up to?

Fool, just a fool.

You remember the disrepect, the tossicity, the unbalance, the fear at every new hero release, especially in the 2 weeks just real money purchase phase.

So why a positive rewiew? Because it's a love-hate relationship.

I love the concept, and I remember the countless match solo and with some few friends that embarked in this train  for the depths of hell with me.

It's almost 10years, and I don't know up untill the handful of devs Ubisoft dispatched to this game will decide to update this chimera.

But i just know, me and all the thounsands of people that tried this, will be sad when the servers gonna shut down.

Still, I ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ hate you For Honor.

If you plan on playing For Honor in 2026 let me break it down for you, grab some popcorn for this one.

Cheaters: This game like any other online PVP title is filled with cheaters, how do you cheat in this game you may ask. Well there's scripters, DDOS attacks, lag switches, and much more that I cant name off the top of my head. Sounds like fun right? well we are just getting started.

Community: I have a total of 1,913 hours on this game from both console and PC, this is one of my most played games on my accounts and I can say with certainty that this is the most toxic, unemployed, cesspit of a community I've ever had to play with. Most seasoned veterans on this game like myself all share the same opinion and yet we still come back too it, why? ill answer that below.

The Games Origins: The game when it released 9 years ago was a huge hit in the industry, nobody had ever made a medieval style fighting game like For Honor which created a small but faithful fanbase for the game gaining about 10,000 players on average for the first year of release. Then 2018 came and the game saw a huge spike in players, the largest its ever had when over 250,000 people joined in August. When September came around the player count had dropped severely to only about 40,000 active players, then it was 20,000 in October, I'm sure you get the idea by now but the game could never retain a large player base. After that single spike the player count has remained at a stagnant 5,000 player average since June 2019, but where did everyone go and why within a 2 month timeframe? 

The Base Game: Your first time playing this game feels great, its a breath of fresh air in the fighting genre. Your first few matches go as expected which is multiple deaths and very few eliminations. You think to yourself, this game has quite the learning curve and you attempt to improve your skills. You learn how to parry heavy's and lights, guard breaks to near perfection, deflecting attacks, dodge timing, bashes, combo strings that would make people weep in a game like chivalry, feats and all 38 character abilities, map knowledge and where the buffs are located, you even 100% the campaign just for the love of the game. All of those skills make you feel like a seasoned player, you probably have close to 1,000-1,500 hours after mastering everything but all of the time and effort ended up being completely pointless when you still try to play a match or two. Due to mechanics like revenge, prop blocking, ladder camping, ledging, or even just connection issues and more, you'll always loose fights that even god himself would say you should have won. 

Matchmaking: I'm in the US-PST zone but the matchmaking expands to each region in my continent and as of each month I've played in 2026 from January to May, the matchmaking has gotten progressively far worse. Just today as of writing this review and many others this year I've had to wait over 25 minutes to find 3 matches. That was for dominion which is the most popular gamemode in For Honor, I personally love breach more which places me in the minority of enjoyers for that mode specifically as the routine matchmaking time is usually about 10-15 minutes for one breach match. The longest I've ever waited for a single game of breach is 25 minutes and that was in February of this year. 

Teammates: Given the average player count is only 5,000 and matchmaking takes up to 15-25 minutes you'd think that the players on your team would also be somewhat seasoned veterans and funny enough most of them are. Most people that I have on my teams throughout my many years of playing all have severe learning curves for their game level that should simply not be present. Level aka reputation does not mean anything in this game, and its the only factor you have to base someone's skill level on before fighting them. It does however give you an idea of how long they've been playing the game, I have acquired almost 200 reputations overall combined on my two accounts and 85% of the time I'm always against or with a team that often exceeds mine by hundreds. It just so happens that I've also been top leaderboard for about 65% of my games played in total despite my team having a far larger amount of time on the game. This is due to multiple factors one being improper team collaboration, i cant stress this enough but EVERY match will have people taking fights that they know results in immediate death leaving one side unbalanced and ultimately causing a loss for the entire team repeatedly. There's also many people who choose to rage quit, go afk, emote instead of fight, blatantly throw a game over ego, or just straight up team kill with crossbows. I have never once had a full set of players on my team that knew how to play the main objective in whatever mode I'm queued for or fight without feeding revenge, this is by far the worst title to solo queue on in video game history.

Ubisoft: their stock price speaks for itself. 

Development Team: My god where do I start. For honor has content that's divided into 4 seasons per year and we get 1 new character every 2 seasons. 95% of the newer characters are absolutely broken for the first few months of release every single time. The Devs realize if they introduce balanced, fair, or creative characters nobody would be willing to pay for them during the two week drop where its locked to only micro transaction access, so they just released the bare minimum while giving the new characters ridiculous abilities or move sets so players would pay 10$ for it, and what do you know IT WORKED. The Devs are also guilty of reworking several iconic characters from the original game and either ruining all originality the character had or just making their move set worse, this also includes maps, feats, attack damage, over bloating files, etc. The main issue with the development team in my opinion is the associate game director Jean-Charles Frenette or as most For Honor players know him JC, he was recorded multiple times even on warriors den (For honor Youtube channel) saying that he could care less what the community wanted in terms of new additions or changes to the game. What a great guy to have as one of the largest dev team voices for the game, he also oversees every update and minor/major change to the game which is probably why its been reduced to a shell of what it once was. I'm also not the kind of guy to point this out but he has severe medical issues like obesity and I'm sure extreme difficulty walking, if you cant take care of yourself what makes you think you can take care of a game that was once enjoyed by over a quarter million players? 

Conclusion: Do not by any circumstances buy this game, if its free and you want to try it out then id recommend the campaign as its a very solid one and bot matches if you really have the urge to keep playing. There is absolutely nothing to gain from playing the PVP modes as you will just end up regretting the time you put into it entirely like myself. I think that I have enough time on this title to give a valid opinion about it, of course there are people who will always disagree. To them I say, how has for honor treated you any differently than it has me? With that I end my review of arguably the worst game I've ever had the misfortune of putting almost 2,000 hours into. If you read the entire review thanks for caring enough, and for the love of god TAKE MY ADVICE.
I like Apollyon and playing as cool dudes in armor and nothing else about this game
this game is like letting the devil into your life. once you play it its hard to stop playing even though the lobbys are from the eternal pits of hell.

I've played this game for a month and I'm already a depressed alcoholic. 
10/10 would recommend!
This game is a blast. I could recommend it when the team balance and hero selection are even, but truthfully, there are times you'll be getting hit like you've never been hit before by your father.

For Honor stands as a strong title that is only limited by a handful of smaller issues and technical hiccups Ubisoft should address. Its story campaign falls short of the best in the genre, and the matchmaking system still needs refinement. Even so, none of that changes the core truth: the game offers some of the deepest and most satisfying combat mechanics to appear in years. For Honor requires real dedication to learn its systems and master them, but players who invest that time will discover an experience that has few equals in gaming today.

It's GG for me Lads.

When I saw the trailer years ago, I always wanted to play this game, but I only managed to do that NOW. And... oh boy, am I disappointed.
The controls are extremely clunky and unresponsive, core game elements like blocking don't even work properly, hit detection is all over the place and the game is infested with paid content. Not so much pay to win, but heavily pay to progress. Both suck.
I could happily ignore that and I don't even mind that there aren't many players anymore, but none of the gameplay goes beyond of just movong to a point, killing someone, moving to another point. It has ZERO depth. 
There is a singleplayer mode, too. And I'd be completely content with that alone - but it's horribly frustrating, too. The arcade matches are 1 minute of clucking around in the UI, then 1 minute loading for max 2 minutes of actual gameplay. That is pretty annoying. Building your heroes is fun, but everything else isn't...

The graphics are nice, no problem with those. The whole lore is absolutely silly, of course, but the heroes are different and interestung enough to completely ignore that. The story mode is completely skip-worthy.

Yeah... spent two evenings downloading those 100 GB. Wasn't really worth it.

Every time I play this game there is a 50/50 chance I hang myself, but the salt I collected playing this game has formed into a shell protecting my body giving me yet another chance to waste my life playing For Honor. 
Thank you Ubisoft and godspeed.

Boot up is a little clunky for Me, but it boots up and runs well after it does its thing.
In case it isnt clear, this is a button combo fighting game and each character plays differently.
I think the combat system is very fun, i like all the character designs and mobility. I also still enjoy playing against the AI and you can still level up against the bots, so im not forced to play insane pvp matches.
 I would like to see different game types and more expansion on the main story in general, but I have fun memories with friends while playing this game.
 Be prepared to lose all the time.  Enjoy the game for what it is, accept the challenge or uninstall it.
Trolls are everywhere, everyone loses eventually, and weather you continue to bash your head against a wall is your choice.  own it.

Can't speak for console players, but most of the negative reviews are due to people getting trolled or spam killed.  Unfortunately, thats just being online with other people.  Little to do with the game itself.  Dead by Daylight is much worse. 
 I put a lot of time in on console and a small chunk of time on pc.  I wouldve put more time into it if they would make the game size less than 115GB.
  The other main thing to remember is that if You look at Steam Charts, the same 4000=6000 people are still playing this game since 2018 and they're the ones you're playing against online. There is not much new players in general, so the game is probably going to match you up against someone who has been playing since release.  You won't win against someone like that.

[h1] My Honest Review [/h1]

[h3] PROS: [/h3]
- Flashy animations, edgy characters and immersive combat is there.
- Character identities are well put. Voice lines and their playstyle are unique.
- Keybinding customization support both for PC and Console is available.
- Local pricing is available on Ubisoft Store for ingame items.
- Lots of customization options for characters
- Lots of room for strategies, team play and micro/macro gameplay with different objectives/win conditions
- The combat feedback feels so good. The flashing guard UI before a hit, the deflect animation with lots of sparks (particle effects) and guardbreaks are all visually satisfying and noticable which makes combat feel smooth.

[h3] CONS: [/h3]
- Learning curve is steep because it is almost like a fighting game. A lot to remember and execute.
- Bot systems are horribly designed. A bot will immediately leave and be replaced with a new connecting player in the middle of combat, revive, or capturing an objective. VERY ANNOYING and UNPREDICTABLE.
- Ubisoft sells heroes in a game about heroes vs heroes. And by pure chance, as luck would have it, new heroes are slightly better/stronger. So you have to pay to buy them or do daily missions for 10-14 days to unlock ONE.
- As long as you have a teammate, something will MOST LIKELY will go wrong, even if you're a really high reputation or ranked player. For example: Someone will interrupt your unique character grab attack (Sohei, Shaman, etc.) and relentlessly fill enemy's Revenge bar so that both you and him can die.
- Game doesn't teach you much, tutorials are outdated. Story Mode abilities and characters abilities and mechanics are irrelevant to current state of the same character in multiplayer mode. (Which is also why nobody really understands how Revenge system works)
- You have to level up each character seperately by leveling them to 7 or 8 reputation to get the best equippable gear for some game modes (Dominion, Breach, etc.) and there are 38 heroes. 1 reputation means 20 levels. You need 20x8x38 minimum to gear all the characters.
- Best way to earn ingame currency (steel) are daily gated Orders and Contracts. Playing nonstop will not yield a lot so your best practice is daily commitment which feels like a chore when you are forced to play something you don't like or simply can't.
- Each cosmetic effect, ornament, color, execution has to be bought seperately regardless of being the same exact thing on every single character. So if you want to execute someone with a wrestling move, you have to buy it for each character. If you want an effect to appear during Execution, Idle, Combat, Free Roam then you have to buy it 4 times PER character. There are also battle passes, boxes for gear and you also pay for the game (which is usually on a %85 discount most of the time). 
- It has some software issues such as Starting as windowed mode no matter how many times I fix it. I press ALT+ENTER to maximize it. It also crashes if you don't update your gpu or use any ingame overlay (Nvidia, Discord, etc)
- There is no reconnect to match option if your game crashes or internet connection is lost.
- There is no dedicated ranked mode with the exception of Ranked 1vs1 Duel (Beta) which is beta since I first played 7 years ago.
- Balance of the characters feel off. Some feel very nerfed and weak while others have access to so much variety in their kit. Warden vs Juren for example as of 05/2026.
- Achievement systems are bugged. They pop on Ubisoft Connect but not on Steam.
- Steam DLC packs do NOT activate instantly in the game due to bugs caused by Ubisoft Connect and Steam will NOT refund the DLCs if you played after buying them regardless of them not activating. They DO NOT care. Buy from Ubisoft Store if you really want to/need to.
- Ubisoft Connect app has security breaches. (Biggest flaw of the game imo)

This game had the potential to be a 10/10 game but Ubisoft's greedy marketing systems lowered it to 6/10 and that 6 coming solely from the fun core combat mechanics. Feel free to play and test game but my advice is do not commit lots of money and expectations into it if you are looking for the perfect medieval pvp combat game.

It's just a worse Chivalry 2. As someone who used to play this game all the time with hundreds of hours on console, its just disappointing. Depending on your hero you start at a heavy disadvantage with it also rewarding spam. You shouldn't have to have really good predictions or reaction speed just to stop someone from spamming light attacks. Hito is another example of brain dead character you see all the time. She has 2 moves, kick, heavy. You either delay the heavy with high damage and hyper armor or just throw it out. They can deal with that? Just tap or hold your kick for a free heavy each time that has hyper armor... Every time I play it doesn't feel fun and fair. It's just people relying on whats easiest or 50/50s. That's pretty much it. It's less of trading blows and more of just gambling with who can predict which version of a move they plan to do and play a game of chicken.

(1000 hours on console)

Great idea. Horrible execution. 

This game is an infuriating grind. The learning curve is steep but after a while you find a character that clicks and you start doing well. This is the best time and the highs are high. The lows are so much lower. Every new character released is over powered and you either have to stop playing or endure until they are nerfed usually to near uselessness. despite there being multiple game modes. Dominion is the only game mode the devs care about. thus the game is balanced around it. 

A game system is made for 1v1 duels is balanced around a 4v4 cluster game mode. Once a certain skill level is reached you honestly can't tell if people are just that cracked or straight up cheating. 

 There are many things to like. Beautiful animation, Great cosmetics with a whole fashion sub community. 

But the game is not worth the drawbacks. Unbalanced gameplay. half the roster is considerably worse than the other, janky hitboxes. toxic community. poor map selection and everything cool requires hours of grinding. 

As much as i would love to love this game. I cannot recommend it.
As a Rainbow Six player, I decided I didn't have enough toxic Ubisoft games in my life and needed more.

For Honor is a game that, once you get the hang of it, truly rocks.

Its combat is heavily skill-based; meaning you won’t get ahead by spamming attacks or luck, you need to master timing, precision, and reading your opponent.

It’s a bit unforgiving and can be frustrating at times, but once you get smooth with it, the feeling of combat is amazing, and every victory feels earned :3

Left at the end of a game. Matchmaking penalty. That's more of a minor annoyance but I still cannot recommend this game to anyone playing for the first time.

The balancing of new characters and the lack of support that this game seems to have had recently has really turned me away from the game. In this game's prime we got so many new characters in such a short amount of time, we got new maps, new gamemodes, and the game felt genuinely fresh for a while after. Now, you're lucky if there is one new (buggy and overpowered) character or a rehashed map from tribute. The only gamemode that has any amount of players is dominion, which tends to get a bit boring after a while. Other gamemodes like deathmatch and tribute are impossible to find a game in. I used to really enjoy this game, and it's a shame that it has become so dull and repetitive. 

(Also ♥♥♥♥ ubisoft for making the new skins cost an absurd amount of money. 20$ for a poorly made render of an assassins creed character? Absolutely not. The mouth animations are genuinely horrifying.)

They also do not care about many characters and it shows given inconsistencies in trade/toolkits clashing causing nicher interactions, as well as fashion. some characters have next to no fashion or good design. shame, so much potential but then again its Ubi.

This game is a russian roulet of For Honor or For Gank. Its fun to play and good complex battles, this also means multiple people can jump you at once. Its infuriating but I still recommend as its fun. Especially with friends!

Not Recommended – 3/10
How the hell do people call this garbage a good game? For Honor is one of the biggest embarrassments Ubisoft has ever released. This game is a complete disaster disguised as a “competitive” AAA title. It takes one of the hardest concepts to ruin  Knights vs Vikings vs Samurai  and turns it into an unbalanced, bug-filled, rage simulator designed to waste your time and destroy your patience. The campaign is atrocious. The story is complete nonsense from beginning to end. Characters exist for no reason, factions have zero depth, the dialogue sounds like it was written in ten minutes, and the game throws random events at you without explaining anything properly. Nothing feels memorable because nothing is written well. Ubisoft tried to make an epic war story and created one of the most lifeless and disconnected campaigns imaginable. The cutscenes are awkward, the pacing is terrible, and by the end you genuinely stop caring about everything happening on screen. The combat is even worse. People praise it because they mistake frustration for “skill.” The animations are horrible. Attacks look broken, movements feel clunky, hitboxes are laughable, and entire fights look like unfinished mocap experiments. This is a game built around reacting to animations, yet the animations themselves are inconsistent garbage. You get hit by attacks that visually miss you, parries fail for no reason, timing feels random, and characters move like stiff robots having seizures. Nothing about the combat feels polished. It feels cheap, messy, and artificially irritating. Then there are the heroes themselves  some of the worst-designed characters Ubisoft has ever made. Half the roster is overloaded with spam, cheap mechanics, and brainless pressure, while other heroes feel completely abandoned and useless. Instead of balance, the game revolves around abusing broken mechanics and exploiting knowledge gaps against newer players. There is no learning curve. There is a brick wall. And the multiplayer? Absolute humiliation. This game is openly designed to torture new players. You buy the game, queue for online, and immediately get thrown against people with thousands of hours who destroy you before you even understand what happened. The matchmaking system is a complete joke. Ranked is meaningless because the playerbase is too small to support real competitive matchmaking. Even longtime players admit the system throws together whoever happens to be online. People complain constantly about waiting forever for matches only to get paired against players massively above their level. Ubisoft failed at creating a real competitive environment and replaced it with a miserable stomp simulator. The online community makes the experience even more pathetic. Toxic players, ego addicts, animation abuse, spam tactics, broken balancing, garbage servers  everything about this game feels hostile. Ubisoft spent years patching this game and still failed to fix its core problems because the foundation itself is rotten. Huge graphics and flashy executions do not save this trainwreck. Underneath the visual effects is a badly designed, exhausting, mechanically inconsistent mess that exists purely to frustrate the player. Ubisoft had an amazing idea and completely destroyed it through incompetence.

DO NOT make the same mistake I made and buy this garbage. Save your money, save your time, and save your sanity.

3/10

10/10 would play it again.

The multiplayer is where this game lives and breathes and if you’re coming from Chivalry expecting the same ragdoll swing fest you’re going to be pleasantly surprised. The directional blocking mechanic creates a genuine mind game between players that feels closer to a fighting game than a medieval brawler, reading your opponent, baiting a parry, committing to an attack knowing they might have the counter ready. That layer of intentionality separates it from anything else in the space.

The hero roster gives it a hero shooter quality that works better in practice than it sounds on paper. Different archetypes with genuinely distinct playstyles Across knight Viking and Sumarai themes create a team dynamic that rewards coordination without demanding it, and finding your character and learning their specific toolkit has that same satisfying groove that good fighting games generate when something finally clicks.

The player pool is smaller than Chivalry which means matches fill reasonably fast but long sessions will have you recognizing faces, sometimes literally the same people rotating through lobbies for an hour straight. It’s the kind of community that forms around a game people genuinely love rather than one that’s simply popular, which has its own charm even if it occasionally feels like a small town.

Haven’t hit the story yet but looking forward to it. 

Worth picking up on sale without hesitation. Great on PC, Steam Deck untested but given Ubisoft’s general optimization history worth checking before committing.

[h1] The good: Occasional fun matches [/h1]

For Honor had a lot of promise when it launched. Ubisoft recently added cross-progression, which is something players had been asking for for years. The game can still be fun when you're evenly matched against players of similar skill and neither team is stacked with the latest overpowered characters, which unfortunately has become a rare occurrence.

Unfortunately, that's where the good news ends.

[h1] The bad: Lazy developers [/h1]

This game is approaching 10 years old and has received almost no meaningful content. There has been virtually no map support whatsoever. The same maps that existed nearly a decade ago are still where you'll be playing your matches today. The gameplay loop is virtually unchanged from what it was years ago, aside from the occasional hero rework. Most of the newer execution animations look clunky, awkward, and noticeably lower quality than many of the older executions.

[h1] The ugly: Balancing hell [/h1]

The real problem, however, is the balancing team: 

New characters routinely launch with absurd kits that make them ridiculously overpowered.

Take Virtuosa, for example. One of the latest characters, she has access to auto-dodges against virtually any attack with 400ms counterattacks, and hyper armor that allows her to ignore what the opponent is doing and still land hits unless she's bashed. Unless your character has instant-access undodgeables, you're going to have a very bad time fighting her, even after her recent "nerfs". And even if your hero technically has undodgeables, many are locked behind chains, that Virtuosa can simply interrupt with her counters before they ever come out. In practice, a large portion of the roster has no reliable answer to her kit, and trying to guardbreak often just gets you bashed or counterattacked instead.

Then there's Juren. The latest hero is packed with full block and delayable dodge attacks (200-800ms), can enter full block in just 100ms and punish you, recover health and stamina through a bash, has instant hyper armor on light attacks, recovery cancels, attack delays, and enough HP and defensive options to punish almost anything. He can also clear an entire minion lane with a single attack. Unlike Virtuosa, your character's moveset barely matters here. Fighting Juren is frustrating regardless of who you play. He is, by far and with no serious competition, the most broken character I've ever seen in a multiplayer video game.

Every new hero seems designed to be stronger and more frustrating to fight than the last, while being extremely easy to use and abuse, likely because it sells and pressures players into buying the newest character. That should give you a pretty good idea of Ubisoft's approach to balance and new content. Rather than creating interesting and balanced additions to the roster, they seem intent on releasing increasingly overloaded heroes that are stronger, safer, and more frustrating to fight than the characters that came before them.

[h1] Review: Not worth your money [/h1]

Unless you're a dedicated player who lives and breathes For Honor, you're heavily punished for taking a break and coming back years later. Learning matchups against newer heroes feels more like homework than entertainment.

Matchmaking doesn't help either. Don't be surprised when you're thrown against players with thousands of hours who know every animation, combo, feint, parry, and punish in the game.

What was once a unique and genuinely exciting fighting game has become a stagnant, poorly balanced experience with one of the most toxic communities you'll encounter online.

If you already own the game, jumping back in for a match or two can still be fun, but can also be incredibly frustrating. It's basically a coin flip: you need an evenly matched game with no OP characters to properly experience the game, which is a rare sight.

If you're considering spending money on For Honor in 2026, just don't. After nearly a decade, there has been very little effort to meaningfully expand the game's core content. Instead, the focus seems to be on selling hero skins and increasingly overloaded characters, which is why this review is marked as do not recommend.

For Honor è senza dubbio uno dei migliori giochi della sua categoria e, per me, uno dei migliori multiplayer competitivi mai realizzati. Lo gioco dal 2018/2019 e, anche se questo non è il mio primo account, sarà almeno il sesto che utilizzo: il fatto che continui a tornarci dopo tutti questi anni dice già tutto sulla qualità del gioco.

Il sistema di combattimento è ancora oggi unico, profondo e soddisfacente, con eroi molto diversi tra loro che richiedono pratica e abilità per essere padroneggiati. Ogni vittoria dà una grande soddisfazione e c'è sempre qualcosa da imparare per migliorarsi.

Nonostante gli anni, For Honor continua a divertirmi come il primo giorno. Pochi giochi riescono a tenermi incollato così a lungo, ed è per questo che lo considero uno dei migliori titoli del suo genere. Consigliatissimo a chi cerca un gioco competitivo, impegnativo e capace di regalare centinaia, se non migliaia, di ore di divertimento.

Only have 70 hours, and believe this is the best melee combat game.
Entirely skill-based, with indeed, a lack of character balance, true.
But if you devote yourself enough, watch and learn instead of rage, you can progress and get stronger.
It's really not a easy game, I'm very far from being at least a "good" player.

However, if you're the kind of player who seek for a easy fighting game, I'd recommend going somewhere else. It's really not a easy game, and takes time to even understand the basics.

I'ma start this review like so:
DO NOT SKIP THE TUTORIAL.
DO NOT SKIP THE STORY.
DON'T EXPECT TEAM WORK.
DON'T EXPECT TO WIN.

For Honor is hard. It's not an easy game. It's not "Just Parry."
It's full of knowledge checks, 50/50s, and unfortunate timing.
Do you have bad internet? Do. Not. Play. This. Game. Your connection matters.
Scripters do exist. The servers do disconnect. Your teammates will abandon you.
Some days you genuinely can't play, everything feels off, and you will mess up all the time.

All that said, For Honor is one of my favorite games of all time. I love the armor, I love the characters, I love the mechanics, I love the story, and I love playing and teaching my friends how to play.
But this game is Niche. Super niche. If you don't like the game, that's fine, there's other games for you. For Honor is a game where winning feels amazing when you do well, and winning when you do bad hurts. And losing when you do well? Even worse. Losing when you're playing horrid? Absolute pain. I've got 1,000 hours here, and another 1,000 on XBOX.
Reputation only matters sometimes. I wish I was joking. I've been cooked by Rep 50s, and slaughtered Rep 1,000s.
There are unspoken rules, tribal knowledge, and more. The community itself? Rough. Not League of Legends rough, not RUST rough, not Valorant rough, but rough. But the kind players that are few and far between are some of the coolest players I've ever met.

This game sucks for anyone who's new to it.

Pros:
Super unique, you'd never see another game like this both combat system and style makes it stand out.

Customisation, it's some of if not the best Customisation in a game making any character look how you like.

The variety in characters is good making this game feel fresh by simply trying someone else.

Cons:

Steep learning curve, not like "deep end of the pool" more like throw you into an active volcano and see what happens kind of learning curve.

Most of the time people win at the character selection screen because some of these new ones are just too much and don't take much skill to play.

It's also a game that's slowly destroying itself with each new character being more op and busted than the last. The devs are trying to fix this broken vase with slamming a hammer onto it with every new patch.

Might be a skill issue ngl but games just gotten boring lately.

I  played on xbox since beta then transitioned to PC recently. This game has consistently catered to incredibly toxic players who play nothing but this game 24/7. I loved this game when it had a more casual audience. Over the years it has devolved to a smaller and more obnoxious group of players who make it their goal to be the reason new players avoid returning to this game. 

Ubisoft fails to balance heroes in ways that make them feel unique and fair, preferring to introduce either a drastically different mechanic that forces people to play sweatier just to compete (Virtuosa) or introducing a hero that is blatantly busted (Juren). These heroes may not be as bad as some others on release, but they're a shining example of the mindset Ubisoft has and what they refuse to account for. Matchmaking is also atrocious as its rare to have a game where you feel its a even fight. Obviously, no games matchmaking is perfect, but For Honor feels like it shifts between placing you in lobbies where its "Baby's first dominion" and "4 stack of meta heroes who have never seen the sun" way too much. I am not going to be one of those cry babies who says "all my matches should be wins!1!1!!!" as that is impossible, but the amount of times ive been put into a lobby where it feels like i offended a rep 100 shinobis bloodline by merely existing is way more then I am able to dismiss.

I really like this game and most likely will continue to play with friends. To any new players or folks who are looking to return: Don't solo Q. Play with friends who are willing to teach you. Be patient and take your time learning. This game is NOT beginner friendly and has not been since year 1.

The prediction based combat changes have ruined this game for me. Used to be fun. Now isn't.
If you're coming back after y2 and before. The game is filled with prediction based mechanics hiding indicators from you until you learn every hero's move sets. Not to mention 10 of the top played hero's have mini-stuns in combat negating the whole purpose of the revenge system and allowing hero's to heavy for free 0 punish. The game as also included a hero that nullifies your lights and heavies. Removed bashes but instead have an entire team of tanks and setups and knockdowns you win. 

Feels bad, plays bad, is bad.

I merged my Xbox and PC accounts so while you can't tell from my stats I have well over a 1000 hours in this game. That being said you can now count my opinion as coming from a casual player of the game... To be brief: The medieval style and look of the maps and armor is very well done, the combat is difficult but extremely rewarding, the game can occasionally seem repetitive, but the complex combat mechanics and regular content updates keep bringing me back. A final note to new players: Give it a chance, we all had to get through the same learning curve, but man has it been some of the most fun, rewarding, and thrilling gameplay of all time.

A truly unique fighting game based on medieval combat with a variety of weapons from swords to spears, hammers, axes etc. A rather steep learning curve that is rewarded with one of the most satisfying experiences in gaming. A skill based game, that is unfortunately bogged down by quite a bit of toxicity from the community. However, there are just as many, if not more, good people who you can befriend and learn with if you are new to the game. Highly addictive and hard to quit.
